content:
The basic idea is to create a tarball and import it. For shipdriver plugins this is simple, just make tarball and then use Options | Plugins | Import Plugin.

It works also with testplugin, but the steps are more convoluted involving a "fake" upload to create the tarball

I do a simple "mkdir build ; cd build; cmake .. ; make"
and then "Import plugin" in OCPN.
This is for shipdriver. tarball is the default target, so make and make tarball is indeed the same thing.
4 messages were moved here from #general > 1tracker_pi by Alec Leamas.
seems a bit inconvenient for development. Sorry if im being obtuse. But before I could use one command "make install" then restart opencpn and the plugin would be loaded, without gui interaction etc

Well... making an omelette wihtout breaking some eggs might be hard.

That said, in the development cycle you con usually update the plugin by justr copying the so/dll file to the proper location (as long as you dont add external data). Making a full import first is the safe route, but after that you can just copy the dll file in your case.
ok I will figure out how to copy it. I was thinknig opencpn could just look in /usr/local/lib/opencpn for plugins first to pick up development ones

Or for us pesky Windows developers...
mkdir build
cd build
cmake .. -A Win32
cmake --build . --config MinSizeRel|RelWithDebInfo|Release|Debug --target tarball
and then just use Plugin Manager to import the plugin.

As Alec suggested, once it has been initially installed, for subsequent builds just copy the newer plugin .dll (or for other platforms .so) into the appropriate directory.

I guess OCPN needs to be restarted after the new dll is copied ?
Not sure if this is faster than just importing the new tarball

@seandepagnier @sean d'epagnier I personally try to cover as much of the code as possible with automated tests. Then just “make test” runs them all. You can see some such tests, coverage reports etc in the weather routing plugin. Details at the bottom of the reader page.

https://github.com/rgleason/weather_routing_pi/blob/master/README.md

It’s obviously not possible to automatically test all the GUI functionality, but it’s pretty straightforward to automatically test non-GUI code. That at least cuts down on how many times you need to restart OpenCPN and do manual testing.

Incidentally, Claude Code AI seems to do a reasonably decent job of writing unit tests for you automatically, if you’re into that sort of thing.

I guess OCPN needs to be restarted after the new dll is copied ?

Yes

Not sure if this is faster than just importing the new tarball

In a scenario where you repeatedly are making changes and recompiles it is definitely faster. But actually no big deal, agreed.
BTW: Not the first time this is discussed: https://github.com/OpenCPN/plugins/issues/1032
A message was moved here from #general > general chat by Alec Leamas.

Sean, We have bash scripts and cmd batch files that build the dll and pdb, copy into msvc and then we debug. Also the script builds relwithdeb and makes a tarball with the metadata added by running the cloudsmith script. These are also used for testing by import in a normal Opencpn. This is using windows tools. The scripts set the path to widgets as well.

There are also scripts to update the template and the opencpn-module from time to time. The CMakeList.txt files have to be compared and changes made for each plugin.
You could look at those scripts and see the comparable for linux.

Also the script builds relwithdeb and makes a tarball with the metadata added by running the cloudsmith script.

This is the only relevant part here. Which script does this, and how should it be invoked to create a tarball which can be imported?

Part pf this is that testplugin docs at https://opencpn-manuals.github.io/main/ocpn-dev-manual/0.1/pm-tp-template.html is missing local build procedure(s)

EDIT: Fix link
